model_findings.description: The new `serve --openai` command defaults to localhost, but `--host` allows binding the unauthenticated proxy to any interface and then passes requests directly to local agent CLIs that run with the user's environment and credentials. That contradicts the documented localhost-only boundary and makes `--host 0.0.0.0` an accidental remote code/cost/credential exposure surface. If non-local binding is supported at all, it should require explicit authentication and prominent opt-in naming; otherwise reject hosts other than loopback.

The chat completions endpoint accepts unbounded `messages` and arbitrary extra request fields, then eagerly flattens all message content into a single prompt before invoking a subprocess. A large local HTTP request can consume substantial memory/CPU during validation and flattening, and for Gemini/Claude/Hermes the prompt is placed directly in argv, which can also exceed OS argument limits. Add explicit request-size/message-count/content-length limits and return a structured 4xx error before constructing the prompt or spawning a backend.

Every chat completion request synchronously spawns a potentially heavy agent CLI with a default 120-second timeout, but the proxy has no concurrency limit, queue, cancellation policy, or per-client throttling. Even on localhost, a misbehaving OpenAI client retry loop can fan out many Codex/Gemini/Claude/Hermes processes and exhaust CPU, memory, file descriptors, or provider quota. Gate backend execution with a small bounded semaphore/queue and return 429/503 when saturated.

The request model allows unknown OpenAI fields but the endpoint silently ignores `stream: true` and still returns a non-streaming JSON response. Many OpenAI-compatible clients switch parsing paths when they request streaming and will expect SSE chunks, so this creates hard-to-debug client failures. Since Phase 1 is intentionally non-streaming, the proxy should explicitly parse `stream` and reject `true` with a clear OpenAI-style 400 error.

The new activity_schema gate silently skips an error-correction activity when items is not a list, and also skips any non-mapping entries inside the list. That means malformed writer output such as items: {incorrect: ...} or a scalar list item can produce passed: true with checked: 0, even though it violates the same item-level schema this gate is meant to enforce and will later be ignored or fail in ActivityParser._parse_error_correction. For a schema gate, non-list items and non-mapping item entries should be reported as violations rather than pass-through cases.
